# RFCs

<!-- Document content (editable) -->

Spectrum Web Components uses an RFC (Request for Comments) process for any change whose shape is worth negotiating before the code lands. Small bug fixes and routine additions do not need an RFC; architectural shifts, cross-component patterns, and documentation-wide reorganizations do.

## When to open an RFC

Open an RFC when the change is likely to:

- Affect more than one component or the framework itself
- Establish or change a pattern other contributors will follow
- Rename, relocate, or remove a public API, package export, or documentation surface
- Introduce a new package, tool, or delivery surface (e.g. `llms.txt`, a new docs site)
- Commit the project to a multi-PR rollout or migration

When in doubt, open a lightweight RFC — a short proposal is cheaper than unwinding a landed change.

## Folders

| Folder | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `proposed/` | RFC is authored, open for comment. |
| `accepted/` | RFC has been agreed to by the team. Implementation PRs may reference it. |
| `superseded/` | RFC was accepted but later replaced by another RFC, or abandoned. |

RFCs move between folders by PR — we do not track state in frontmatter.

## Process

1. **Author.** Copy [`template.md`](template.md) into `proposed/` as `YYYY-MM-DD-short-title.md`. Fill in every required section; `N/A` is an acceptable answer when the section truly doesn't apply.
2. **Open a PR.** Target the default branch. Use the PR description for meta-discussion ("this is an early draft; looking for directional feedback"), not for RFC content — the RFC file is the durable artifact.
3. **Collect feedback.** In PR comments, in `spectrum-web-reviews` Slack, at team sync, or at office hours. Longer-running RFCs often use all four.
4. **Revise.** Update the RFC in place; note revisions with commit messages, not inline changelog entries.
5. **Accept or reject.** A reviewer (or the author) moves the file from `proposed/` to `accepted/` (or closes the PR without merging, or moves it to `superseded/` if another RFC replaces it).

## RFCs that live on a branch

Some RFCs are proposed on a working branch and kick off a body of work before they merge. That is fine — commit the RFC to the branch, link to it from implementation PRs, and merge the RFC to `main` alongside (or ahead of) the first implementation PR. The RFC template and process are the same.

## Naming

`YYYY-MM-DD-kebab-case-title.md` — date is the RFC's first commit, not its acceptance date. Titles are short and descriptive (`focus-management-strategy`, `docs-overhaul-v2`, `swc-button-variants`).

## Format

Every RFC uses the sections in [`template.md`](template.md). Do not reorder sections; readers and tooling rely on the order. Add subsections inside any section as needed.

Breadcrumbs and TOC are generated by `update-nav.js` — do not hand-edit them.
